title: Recognising stress in pigs
subtitle: Behavioural and physical signs that pigs are not coping.
excerpt: Pigs under stress show it through behaviour before they show it through physical signs. A pig that is tail-biting, bar-biting, or standing apart from the group is under environmental, social, or health pressure. Identifying the cause early prevents escalation and meets UK welfare code obligations.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Pigs, gov.uk. AHDB Better Returns Programme pig welfare guide. Grandin, T., Livestock Handling and Transport (2014), standard reference for welfare-based observation.

glossaryTerms.definition: A vice behaviour in pigs where one pig bites the tail of another. A welfare indicator for overcrowding, boredom, nutritional deficiency, or social stress. Can cause serious injury quickly.
Pigs communicate through a range of calls. Sustained high-pitched squealing, screaming, or continuous grunting that is unusual for the group is a sign of distress.

content.text: Pigs are intelligent social animals. They show discomfort through behaviour before physical signs appear. Ten minutes of daily watching is enough to spot problems while they are still fixable.
All pigs eating together at feeding time. A pig that hangs back at feeding may be being bullied or is unwell.
All tails intact and uncurled. A straight, still tail is a warning sign. A curled, wagging tail is a relaxed, active pig.
No pig separated from the group for more than a few minutes, unless she has chosen a comfortable sleeping spot. A pig lying alone and not moving with the group when they feed is often unwell.
, contented grunting, quiet squeals when food is brought. Sustained screaming or continuous distressed calling is an emergency.

is a group welfare failure, not a character flaw in one pig. The biting pig is usually a symptom of a wider problem. Common causes: overcrowding, too little feeding space, high temperatures, poor enrichment, or low salt in the diet.

Remove the injured pig immediately. A blood-smell in a pig group will attract more biting from multiple animals. Treat the wound, keep the injured pig separate until healed, and investigate the cause before returning her. If you cannot identify and correct the cause, the biting will resume.

A pig that chews the gate bars, shakes its head for no reason, or paces the same route is showing a stereotypy. This is a sign of long-term frustration or boredom. It develops when pigs lack room to root, enough space, or company.

Enrichment reduces stereotypy: straw, branches, a suspended chain or rope, a shallow tray of soil to root in. Outdoor pigs with access to rooting ground rarely develop stereotypies; housed pigs without enrichment commonly do.

UK law requires all pigs to have permanent access to materials they can root and manipulate. Straw in sufficient quantity to allow normal rooting behaviour is the most practical enrichment for a small holding. A single twist of straw per pig per day is not enough; a thick bedded area they can dig into is the target.
